Dark Scene refers to a scene where the light is too low to distinguish the target.
Overexposure occurs when the illumination is too high to distinguish the target.
Illumination Change indicates illumination changes during one video sequence.
Out of View refers to the object partially or fully leaving the view.
Fast Motion is defined as the average per-frame object motion being larger than 20 pixels.
Target Rotation involves the target rotating in-plane or out of plane.
Scale Variation refers to the ratio change of target size between the minimum and maximum being more than 50%.
Deformation indicates the object is deformable.
Composite Objects are an ensemble of multiple objects, as an example, a man with a basketball.
Low Resolution occurs when the ratio of the object area to the image size is lower than 5%.
Motion Blur happens when the target is blurred due to its own motion or camera motion.
Viewpoint Change refers to the viewpoint not being fixed because of changes in the capturing angle.
Sensor Failure occurs when at least one camera cannot provide useful information.

These two concepts can be fused using weights for early fusion, as described in the context. They go through convolution, batch norm, and global pooling layers to extract features and context, followed by fully connected layers to output importance values. Finally, they are fused by importance weights.
The RGB image patch is used in the process to calculate Xrgb, which involves global pooling, convolution, batch norm, and fully connected layers.
The depth image patch is used in the process to calculate Xdepth, which involves global pooling, convolution, batch norm, and fully connected layers.
Xrgb and Xdepth are concatenated and passed through fully connected layers to output importance values (α and β), and then alpha times Xrgb and beta times Xdepth are fused together to get Xfused.
Xfused has the same size as the original images after the fusion process.
